coredata.dc:title: The effects of DMAEMA polyelectrolyte and TiO2 photocatalyst on the tartrazine quenching of N-doped carbon dot immobilized in PVA/microfibrillated cellulose film
coredata.dc:creator: Ng, H.K. Melvin
Lim, G.K.
Leo, C.P.
coredata.dc:description: The quenching of carbon dots (CDs) is affected by surface functional groups and other factors. Since CDs were also immobilized into thin films as a sensor, the effects of film properties on their photoluminescence should be investigated. In this work, N-doped CDs (NCDs) were immobilized in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin films for tartrazine detection. Using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, amorphous NCDs were successfully produced. NCDs were blended into the PVA dope solution containing microfibrillated cellulose (MFC). An excessive amount of NCDs caused the undesired interaction and mutual reabsorption between the NCDs, resulting in self-quenching. Dimethylaminoethyl acrylate (DMAEMA) was added as copolymer to reduce the swelling ratio by about 50%. The reduction of water uptake subsequently reduced the tartrazine adsorption and the quenching of NCDs. Contradictory, the addition of TiO2 nanoparticles improved the tartrazine adsorption and the NCDs quenching. The thin film also exhibits photocatalytic properties for analyte removal. The linear ΔF and the unchanged absorbance spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film indicate dynamic quenching at low tartrazine concentration (< 10 µM), attaining a limit of detection of 4.66 µM. The overlapping spectrum between the analyte absorbance and NCDs film emission spectra showed possible inner filter effects or fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Microfibrillated cellulose
Polyvinyl alcohol
Tartrazine adsorption
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and photoluminescent thin films
The effects of copolymerization with weak polyelectrolyte
The effects of TiO2 nanoparticles incorporation
Quenching mechanism of the photoluminescent thin film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge Ministry of Education Malaysia for supporting this research via Program Translational MRUN-Rakan RU (MRUN − Rakan RU-2019–004/2 , 203 , PJKIMIA/6720018 ). Finally, we would like to thank Borregaard Cellulose Fibrils for providing MFC. Universiti Sains Malaysia is acknowledged for providing Graduate Assistant Scheme to Mr. Melvin Ng Hau Kwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image (b) XRD pattern of NCDs.
(a) Transmittance percentage, (b) PL spectra, and (c) the overlapping of transmittance percentage at 350 nm and PL spectra of films at 430 nm under UV.
(a) Transmittance percentage and (b) PL spectra of films with varied PVA to DMAEMA ratio.
PL of spectra of (a) PVA/DM0/NCD, (b) PVA/DM1/NCD, (c) PVA/DM2/NCD, and (d) PVA/DM3/NCD before (solid line) and after (dashed line) tartrazine adsorption.
(a) Adsorption kinetics, (b) transmittance spectra, and (c) PL spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 before and after tartrazine adsorption (dashed line).

(a) PL of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 after various tartrazine concentration adsorption (b) ΔF of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 after various tartrazine concentration adsorption at 430 nm. (Inset: The linear plot at low concentration).

(a) PL spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film before and after tartrazine adsorption and UV cleaning with varied time, (b) F/F
o of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film at 430 nm with varied time (0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h); (c) PL spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film after UV cleaning for 4 h with multiple cycles; (d) F/F
o of PVA/DM3/NCDs/MFC/TiO2 film after UV cleaning for 4 h at 430 nm with multiple cycles.
Normalized PL of (a) PVA/DM3/NCD and (b) PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2; absorption spectra of tartrazine samples after adsorption using (a) PVA/DM3/NCD and (b) PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2.
(a) PL spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film after tartrazine and MB adsorption, (b) normalized PL spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film, absorbance spectra of tartrazine (10 µM) and MB (10 µM).
The composition of the polymer dope solution.
Comparison of performance of CDs on tartrazine detection.

head.ce:title: The effects of DMAEMA polyelectrolyte and TiO2 photocatalyst on the tartrazine quenching of N-doped carbon dot immobilized in PVA/microfibrillated cellulose film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14300 Nibong Tebal, Pulau Pinang, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14300 Nibong Tebal, Pulau Pinang, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 11800 Penang, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Sciences, Universiti Sains Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 11800 USM, Penang, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The quenching of carbon dots (CDs) is affected by surface functional groups and other factors. Since CDs were also immobilized into thin films as a sensor, the effects of film properties on their photoluminescence should be investigated. In this work, N-doped CDs (NCDs) were immobilized in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin films for tartrazine detection. Using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, amorphous NCDs were successfully produced. NCDs were blended into the PVA dope solution containing microfibrillated cellulose (MFC). An excessive amount of NCDs caused the undesired interaction and mutual reabsorption between the NCDs, resulting in self-quenching. Dimethylaminoethyl acrylate (DMAEMA) was added as copolymer to reduce the swelling ratio by about 50%. The reduction of water uptake subsequently reduced the tartrazine adsorption and the quenching of NCDs. Contradictory, the addition of TiO2 nanoparticles improved the tartrazine adsorption and the NCDs quenching. The thin film also exhibits photocatalytic properties for analyte removal. The linear ΔF and the unchanged absorbance spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film indicate dynamic quenching at low tartrazine concentration (< 10 µM), attaining a limit of detection of 4.66 µM. The overlapping spectrum between the analyte absorbance and NCDs film emission spectra showed possible inner filter effects or fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained widespread interest in sensing science due to their photoluminescence properties that are useful in the detection of hazardous materials [1,2]. More importantly, they can be easily synthesized from green chemicals, and biomass using top-down or bottom-up methods. The quenching mechanisms have also been extensively studied [3,4]. Static and dynamic quenching mechanisms are caused by the return of electrons to the ground state in CDs, resulting in no emission after light absorption. CDs-analyte complex initiates the static quenching while the collision between CDs and analyte induces dynamic quenching. The dynamic quenching can be further confirmed by the linear reduction of photoluminescence after increasing the analyte concentration. In addition, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effects (IFE), and more have been studied.

In the detection of tartrazine, Xu et al. [5] suggested that static quenching of CDs instead of dynamic quenching is based on the quenching constant calculated from the Stern−Volmer equation decreased with the rise of temperature. The detection is not only affected by CDs amount, but also the reaction temperature and solution pH. The effects of temperature had not been explained, but the effects of solution pH were correlated to the changes of photoluminescence due to the variation of functional groups on CDs such as hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties. Chatzimitakos et al. [6] synthesized CDs with oxygen and nitrogen-rich functional groups for tartrazine detection as well. However, the quenching of CDs in the detection of tartrazine was found not affected by solution pH, unlike Fe3+ detection. The pH buffer is used to stabilize the solution pH at 3 to avoid iron hydrolysis. Other quenching mechanisms can be validated from the overlapping comparison between the excitation spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of the analyte, which was caused by the inner filter effect (IFE). The effectiveness of IFE is reflected by the extent of the spectra overlapping area. The sizeable overlapping area denotes the domination of IFE in quenching. Liu et al. [7] postulated that more than one quenching mechanism could occur simultaneously. Thulasi et al. [8] confirmed that the static quenching of CDs from vehicle exhaust soot occurs with IFE in the detection of tartrazine using the time-resolved fluorescence method. Although these CDs with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups were quenched by Fe3+ ions as well, potassium thiocyanate was added to eliminate the quenching by Fe3+ through complex formation. Besides IFE, electron transfer resulted from the strong interactions between analytes and the functional groups of the N-CDs can contribute to FRET quenching as well. FRET quenching involves the light emission from CDs with spectrum overlapping with the absorbance spectrum of analyte at a small distance between 1 and 10 nm. Li et al. [9] postulated that vitamin B/tartrazine worked as the acceptor of energy transferred from N,S-CDs that were quenched without shift at its maximum emission wavelength, even the concentration of vitamin B/tartrazine was increased. A similar finding of FRET quenching of CDS was also reported where N, Cl co-doped CDs were applied to detect tartrazine in beverages [10]. The average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is required to determine the distance between the receptor and the donor for the confirmation of FRET quenching [10].

Besides of CDs solution, researchers also studied the immobilization of CDs in thin films for practical uses [11–14]. However, they focused on the detection of tetracycline [14], dopamine [11], and glucose [12]. To our best knowledge, the study on the detection of tartrazine using the immobilized CDs has not been reported. The detection of the anionic azo dye, tartrazine is equivalently crucial as it is commonly used in food and cosmetic. The overdose of tartrazine may lead to allergies, skin rashes, asthma, and more health problems [15]. In our previous work [16,17], the photoluminescent thin film was improved for the tartrazine sensor by incorporating nitrogen modified CDs (NCDs) into the three-dimensional network of microfibrillated cellulose (MFC) in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) thin film. The luminescence intensity was improved due to the improvement of NCDs dispersion. Nevertheless, the effects of thin-film properties on the quenching of CDs have not been studied to our best knowledge. The understanding of such effects is essential since different polymer thin film interacts with analyte differently, depending on their properties such as wetting and sorptivity.

In this work, the amount of NCDs in PVA thin film was first varied to study the effects of NCDs loading on the characteristics of PVA thin film and the tartrazine quenching. The PVA/NCDs films with satisfactory photoluminescence, light transmittance, and tartrazine quenching were further incorporated with a weak polyelectrolyte, (2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (DMAEMA). DMAEMA crosslinking can reduce water absorption and film swelling. Lastly, TiO2 nanoparticles were blended into PVA to improve analyte adsorption and to create reusability. The possible quenching mechanism of the film was discussed as well.

Citric acid (≥99%, Merk), and urea (≥ 99.5%, Merk) were used to synthesis NCDs. Microfibrillated cellulose (MFC, Exilva F01-L) sponsored by Borregaard Cellulose Fibrils was utilized in the preparation of photoluminescent composite. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA with an average molecular weight of 31,000–50,000, 87–89% hydrolyzed), DMAEMA, N,N′-Methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA) (99%), and N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) (≥ 99.5%) were acquired from Sigma while Ammonium persulfate (APS) (≥98%) was supplied by Merck. These chemicals were used in the preparation of the durable composite film. Ethanol (≥99.4%) and methylene blue were supplied by Merck while tartrazine was acquired from R&M Chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and photoluminescent thin films
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs precursor was prepared by dissolving 1.051 g of citric acid and 0.3 g of urea into 10 mL of distilled water [18]. 10 mL of the precursor solution was stirred using a magnetic stirrer (WiseStir MSH – 20D) at 300 rpm for 30 min. The solution was then heated in a microwave (Samsung MW71e) at 750 W for 150 s [19]. The resulting brownish solid was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water with stirring for another 1 h at 300 rpm. Centrifugation was carried out at 4000 rpm for 30 min (Eppendorf 5702R) to remove suspended solids, and the supernatant was further purified using a 0.45 µm syringe filter to remove large agglomerate [19,20].

The polymeric thin film was prepared as followed. MFC was added into the NCDs solutions and stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the solutions were heated to 90 °C and PVA was added slowly under stirring at 500 rpm. Citric acid was added into the mixture as a cross-linker. The mixture was continuously stirred for 4 h until PVA and citric acid were completely dissolved and dispersed [21]. The composition of the dope solution with different amounts of NCDs solutions was prepared according to the list in Table 1.

The dope solution was degassed using a planetary mixer (Kurabo KK-300SS) with 1 min mixing at 1000 rpm followed by 5 min degassing at 1000 rpm to eliminate the air bubble and sedimentation. The photoluminescent thin films were prepared using a casting machine (XB320D) with a gap of 500 µm and a speed of 1.6 cm/s. These films were dried overnight. The thin films were peeled off and cured at 140 °C for 4 h so that the film does not dissolve in water.

The PVA thin film with an appropriate amount of NCDs was then modified by adding the different concentrations of DMAEMA as copolymer. DMAEMA and MBA were added to the prepared dope solution and stirred at 300 rpm for 30 min. The solution was purged with pure N2 at 20 mL/min for 10 min. Then, 10% (w/V) APS and 5% (V/V) TEMED were added to initiate the polymerization. The solution was stirred for another 24 h before degassing and casting, as stated above. The copolymerized thin film with a satisfactory trade-off of PL and light transmittance was then incorporated with TiO2 nanoparticles to enhance the adsorption and photocatalytic effects.

The NCDs were diluted with ethanol and dropped on a copper grid coated with holey carbon film. The microstructure of NCDs samples was studied using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss Libra 120) after ethanol evaporation.

The XRD analysis of NCDs was carried out using an Advance diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8, Germany) to determine the structure of NCDs. The XRD scanning was performed in the 2θ range of 10–90 with a step of 0.03°.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of photoluminescent thin films were collected using FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10) in the range of 4000–650 cm−1. The transparency of the film in terms of light transmittance within a range of 200–1000 nm through the thin films was measured using was a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Carry 60, Agilent). The photoluminescence of these thin films was measured using the fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer LS55), with the aid of a solid sample holder accessory with a 1% light transmittance setting. The excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm. The scanning was conducted at a scan speed of 1000 nm per min as well as at the excitation and emission widths of 15 nm each. The emission was recorded from 300 nm to 798.5 nm.

The swelling ratio of the films before and after the addition of DMAEMA was determined by immersing 1 cm × 1 cm of the films in 20 mL of distilled water for 30 min. The swelling ratio was calculated as followed.
(1)
Swelling ratio =
w −
w
o
w
× 100 %
where w
o is the dry weight of film while w is the weight of the film after absorption. Similarly, the surface area of the films was measured using vernier caliper and calculated by
(2)
Surface area ratio =
A −
A
o
A
× 100
%
where A
o is the surface area of the film before absorption while A is the weight of the film after absorption.

The sensing properties of these photoluminescent thin films were tested using 10 mL of tartrazine solution with a concentration of 10 μM. After immersing the thin films into tartrazine solution for 30 min, the thin films were dried in an oven at 45 °C for 1 h, followed by quenching studies using the fluorescence spectrometer. The film recovery test was carried out under UV irradiation in 20 mL distilled water within varied duration (0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, and 4 h) up to 5 cycles.

The equilibrium adsorption of tartrazine on PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film was also determined. The tartrazine concentration at different time intervals was determined using a UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent, Carry 60) at a wavelength of 430 nm. The adsorption kinetic models, namely the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models, were used to understand the adsorption.
(3)
log (
Q
e
−
Q
t
) =
log
Q
e
−
k
1
2.303
t
(4)
t
Q
t
=
1
k
2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q
e
where t is the time is taken (min), Q
t is the amount of tartrazine removed at time t (mg/gcat), Q
e is the adsorption capacity at equilibrium (mg/gcat), k
1 is the rate constant of the pseudo-first-order model (min−1), and k
2 is the rate constant of the pseudo-second-order model (g·mgcat −1·min−1), respectively.

NCDs with spherical shape and a diameter of about 10 nM are shown in the HRTEM image ( Fig. 1(a)). The absence of fringes in the HRTEM image positively indicates the amorphous structure of these NCDs. The amorphous structure was further proven in the XRD analysis, where a broad peak around 24° was obtained Fig. 1(b), indicating a (002) plane of graphitic carbon structure [20,22,23]. A similar observation on HRTEM images and XRD pattern was reported by others when the same microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was used to synthesize NCDs [24,25]. Thomas et al. [23] further support the carbon graphitic structures formed by the calculated ratio of sp3 carbon (D-band) to sp2 carbon (G-band) obtained from Raman spectroscopy.

Polymer thin films with a dense structure formed after curing, as reported in our previous work [17]. The changes in film morphology are expected to be insignificant since a small amount of NCDs has been incorporated. By increasing the amount of NCDs solution in the dope solution, the resulted polymer films showed a reduction in light transmittance ( Fig. 2). The light transmittance of PVA/NCD thin film at 350 nm was nearly 0% since the thin film lost its transparency by adding the dark brown NCD solution. A similar observation was reported when 1 wt% of CDs were added into Slygard film [26] as well as when NCDs were added into PVA film [27]. The reduction of film transparency, especially in the UV region was due to NCDs aggregation in the thin film. The aggregation could cause light scattering and redshift of wavelength in the emission spectrum [26]. On the other hand, the photoluminescence of the film was improved by increasing the NCD amount from 5% to 20%. The increasing amount of NCDs in PVA films caused the improvement of light excitation and emission. However, the photoluminescence started to drop significantly as the NCDs amount was further increased from 20% to 100%. The drop could be related to the shorter distance between the NCDs at a higher amount [27]. More interaction and mutual reabsorption between NCDs resulted in self-quenching and hence reduced the photoluminescence intensity [27]. Since PVA/0.2NCD thin film showed the highest photoluminescence with satisfactory translucent, it was selected for further modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of copolymerization with weak polyelectrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The hydroxyl groups of PVA are useful for chemical crosslinking to alter the film properties [28]. In this work, DMAEMA was crosslinked with PVA to reduce the water absorption and film swelling. PVA was copolymerized with DMAEMA at various weight ratio (1: 0.06, 1: 0.12, 1: 0.24). The FTIR spectra of copolymer films (PVA/DM1/NCD, PVA/DM2/NCD, PVA/DM3/NCD) in Fig. S1 shows that all the peaks remained similar to PVA film (PVA/0.2NCD). PVA film possesses an abundance of –OH functional group, C–H stretching of alkane, and C˭O group which were represented by the broad spectrum within 3000–3600 cm−1, peak around 2840–3000 cm−1 and sharp peak around 1630 cm−1, respectively [29,30]. The N-H peak of DMAEMA could not be determined due to peak overlapping since amine bending and amine stretching of DMAEMA are normally located at 1650 cm−1, 2800–3000 cm−1, and 3300–3400 cm−1 [28].

Without the presence of DMAEMA, the PVA/DM0/NCD thin film absorbed water and swelled significantly, with a swelling ratio of 40.8% and the surface area ratio of 44.0% (Fig. S2). The film swelled because of the relaxation of the amorphous region, while the water uptake was contributed by the abundance of hydroxyl groups in the film [29]. By incorporating the various amount of DMAEMA, the swelling ratio dropped to 24.3–21.3%, while the surface area dropped to 21.0%. The drop in swelling ratio and surface area ratio indicated that the water absorption of the membrane was reduced. The copolymerization of DMAEMA with PVA caused the thin film to be rigid due to the reduction of hydroxyl groups [29] and the rising electrostatic repulsion between the charged polymers [31].

Tartrazine adsorption by PVA/DMA1/NCD and PVA/DMA2/NCD thin films had not been significantly improved at a low copolymerization ratio of DMAEMA (weight ratio of 0.06 and 0.12) in comparison to PVA/DM0/NCD thin film. Even with the highest weight ratio of 1: 0.24, only 0.1% of tartrazine was adsorbed by PVA/DMA3/NCD thin film. The low concentration of DMAEMA limited the cationic effect of DMAEMA to improve the interaction with tartrazine. Further increment of DMAEMA would lead to oversaturated polymer solution that resulted in a brittle thin film that is not suitable for practical uses.

The copolymerization with DMAEMA reduced the film transparency slightly, as shown in Fig. 3(a). More crosslinking occurred due to the formation of the copolymer, resulting in the disorganized polymer matrix. Xu et al. [32] also reported the transparency reduction of PVA composite film when CDs were added. The transmittance of their PVA/CQDs film dropped to near zero in the middle UV region when 4 mL of CDs was added [32]. Fig. 3(b) exhibits the reduction of PL intensity of PVA/DM1/NCD, PVA/DM2/NCD, and PVA/DM3/NCD thin films with the introduction of copolymer DMAEMA. The slight reduction of PL intensity might be caused by the transmittance reduction for light absorbance or light scattering [33].

All the films were then quenched with tartrazine. PVA/DM1/NCD and PVA/DM2/NCD thin films showed a reduction in PL intensity and attained ΔF at 430 nm of 16.81 and 15.96, respectively ( Fig. 4). The reduction of PL intensity is considerably low compared to PVA/DM0/NCD thin film, which achieved ΔF of 60.30 at 430 nm. The reduction in the ΔF was due to the drop in water uptake by PVA/DM1/NCD and PVA/DM2/NCD thin films, which in turn reduced tartrazine adsorption. However, the ΔF of PVA/DM3/NCDs/MFC was slightly increased to 25.48 in comparison to PVA/DM1/NCD and PVA/DM2/NCD thin films. The increment might have resulted from the cationic effects of DMAEMA [34]. The changes in tartrazine absorption due to the changes in film properties affect the quenching because it reduces the collision between CDs and analyte and/or the charge transfer for quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of TiO2 nanoparticles incorporation
ce:section.ce:para:
TiO2 nanoparticles were further incorporated into PVA/DM3/NCD thin film to improve tartrazine adsorption for quenching. With the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles, the PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film adsorbed tartrazine up to 40.60% within 30 min. An adsorption capacity at equilibrium, Q
e,calc of 1.28 mg/g was recorded as well. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the tartrazine adsorption by PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film fitted pseudo-second order model with a rate constant, k of 0.0114 g/mg·min which is better than pseudo-first order model. The porous TiO2 nanoparticles provided a large surface area for the adsorption of tartrazine [35]. The light transmittance reduced significantly (Fig. 5(b)) with the presence of TiO2 as it absorbed most of the light, especially in the UV region as observed by others [36]. Nevertheless, the reduction of light transmittance did not reduce the PL intensity (Fig. 5(c)). The enhancement of tartrazine adsorption aids to improve the sensitivity of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film. The change in PL, ΔF of 72.07 was documented, which is about three times more compared to PVA/DM3/NCD thin film without TiO2 (Fig. 5(c)). The quenching of NCDs was greatly enhanced by tartrazine adsorption due to the promotion of CDs-analyte complex formation or the collision between CDs and analyte. The photo taken under UV light analyzed in term of RGB using ImageJ analysis also supported the PL spectroscopy result of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2, showing the reduction of total grey pixel intensity (Fig. S3).

However, PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film only exhibited linear quenching at low tartrazine concentration (<10 µM) when it was further quenched with varied concentration of tartrazine ( Fig. 6). The linear quenching pattern shown by NCDs solution within a wide range of tartrazine concentrations (0–100 µM) [17] could not be attained. In addition, the increment of tartrazine concentration beyond 10 µM did no further diminish the PL intensity, indicating the maximum detected concentration to be 10 µM. The observation could be related to the improved tartrazine adsorption, leading to a more significant quenching effect at a lower concentration. Table 2 summarizes the results of tartrazine detection using CDs, N,Cl-CDs, CDs immobilized in the polymeric thin film, and NCDs immobilized in the polymeric thin film. It is important to note that the limits of detection (LODs) of CDs and NCDs immobilized in the polymeric thin film are higher compared to the LODs of CDs and N,Cl-CDs in aqueous solution. Nevertheless, the higher ΔF at the lower concentration represents a better sensitivity compared to our previous work [16], improving from a LOD of 28.55 μM to a LOD of 4.66 µM (2.49 ppm).

The photocatalytic properties of the films were further evaluated to seek the possibility of reuse. PVA/DM3/NCD thin film exhibited photocatalytic properties even without the presence of TiO2 (Fig. S4) as the PL recover about 85% after UV cleaning for 4 h. NCDs possess photocatalytic property as reported by others [37–39]. With the additional aids of TiO2 nanoparticles, 71.5% of PL intensity of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film could be recovered up just after 30 min of UV cleaning. It even managed to recover further by increasing UV cleaning time, with 100% PL recovery after 4 h ( Fig. 7(a) and (b)). The photocatalytic cleaning was promoted by the facilitated electron transfer and the reduced recombination of the electron when coupling of NCDs and TiO2 [38,39]. These electron-hole pairs generated could oxidize water molecules and produce OH radicals [40]. The radicals oxidized and mineralized the tartrazine molecules adsorbed, recovering the photoluminescent properties of the film. The reusability was further tested with multiple runs, and it was found that the recovery of PL intensity reduced slightly as the number of adsorption and UV cleaning cycles increased (Fig. 7(c) & (d)). The irreversible poisoning or the fouling of the active sites on the TiO2 possibly occurred [41]. The cost of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film was estimated to be about USD 0.02 per piece only based on the bulk chemical price. The reuse of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film as a sensor is optional since it is very low in cost.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of the photoluminescent thin film
ce:section.ce:para: After the copolymerization with weak polyelectrolyte and the addition of TiO2, the thin film turned from transparent to opaque, as shown in Fig. 5(b). However, the films still possess a photoluminescent response due to the presence of NCD. Thus, the synergy effect between TiO2 and NCDs [42] is expected to remain after immobilization in the polymeric matrix. TiO2 absorbed most of the light, especially in the UV region, and then transferred the photon to NCDs for excitation and emission.

The normalized absorption spectra of adsorbed tartrazine and PL spectra of the films were plotted together in Fig. 8. As mentioned earlier, TiO2 nanoparticles in PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film improved tartrazine adsorption, which in turn increased the overlapping region of the absorption spectra of tartrazine and PL spectra of the film. IFE or FRET quenching further explained the improved quenching (ΔF) of the PVA/DM3/NCD /TiO2 thin film [9,10]. The linear ΔF in Fig. 6(b) also showed that dynamic quenching occurred at low tartrazine concentration (<10 µM) [3]. There were no changes in the absorption spectra of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film (Fig. S5), especially the absorbance peak of tartrazine at 430 nm. The absence of an extra peak after tartrazine adsorption, further indicating no complex was formed for static quenching [10].

The quenching effects of another analyte, namely methylene blue (MB) on PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film were further studied. As seen in Fig. 9(a), the PL of PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film only dropped slightly after MB adsorption. This was because the overlapping absorption spectra of MB is small compared to that of tartrazine. The film even shows a similar quenching effect when the film is quenched by tartrazine with and without MB. PVA/DM3/NCD/TiO2 thin film is highly selective as long the overlapping region of the absorption spectra of competitive analytes does not fall between the PL spectra of the film.

NCDs with the amorphous structure in nanosize were produced in a microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis route. They were used to fabricate photoluminescent PVA/MFC thin films via a simple blending-casting method. The increased NCDs loading in PVA/MFC thin films caused the decline of transmittance and the increment of PL intensity. However, the excessive loading of NCDs caused PL intensity to reduce. The reduction was caused by the self-quenching of NCDs after the interaction and the mutual reabsorption between the NCDs. Copolymerization with DMAEMA reduced the swelling of PVA/MFC thin films incorporated with NCDs. However, the reduction of water uptake diminished tartrazine adsorption and subsequently, the quenching of NCDs. The following blending of TiO2 nanoparticles into the photoluminescent thin films promoted tartrazine adsorption, which in turn improved the quenching of NCDs in the thin films. The presence of TiO2 nanoparticles also enabled the photocatalytic removal of tartrazine from the thin films, which enable the thin film to recover its PL after UV cleaning.

H.K. Melvin Ng: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. G.K. Lim: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. C.P. Leo: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Ministry of Education Malaysia for supporting this research via Program Translational MRUN-Rakan RU (MRUN − Rakan RU-2019–004/2, 203, PJKIMIA/6720018). Finally, we would like to thank Borregaard Cellulose Fibrils for providing MFC. Universiti Sains Malaysia is acknowledged for providing Graduate Assistant Scheme to Mr. Melvin Ng Hau Kwan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2020.104850.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
